response: The global food system faces mounting pressure from a projected population of 9.7 billion by 2050—which will require 60% more food—while climate change reduces crop yields and agricultural activity itself contributes roughly 25% of greenhouse gas emissions. These challenges are compounded by severe water scarcity in major farming regions, soil degradation affecting 33% of the world’s arable land, and the loss or waste of about one-third of all food produced. Proposed solutions range from precision agriculture, vertical farming, and alternative proteins to genetically modified drought-resistant crops, regenerative farming, and policy reforms around subsidies and carbon pricing. However, the central challenge is implementing these innovations at scale while ensuring food remains affordable and accessible to the world’s poorest populations.
